Call 2024 Round 3 YOU
KA152-YOU - Mobility of young people (KA152-YOU)
Form ID KA152-YOU-385B050D Deadline (Brussels Time) 08 Oct 2024 12:00:00
Project Summary
Please provide short answers to the following questions to summarise the information you have provided in the rest of the
application form.
Please use full sentences and clear language. In case your project is accepted, the summary you provided will be made
public by the European Commission and the National Agencies.
What do you want to achieve by implementing the project? What are the objectives of your project? Please specify from the
perspective of youth work practice.
Earth and You(th) was conceived with the primary objective of making young people more aware of the climate changes that
are occurring in our environmental system, what the main triggers are and what each of us can do to reduce these
consequences. The need for this project was raised by the participants themselves, who expressed a desire to tackle their
local environmental challenges while learning about international initiatives. Our project objectives align closely with the
principles of youth work, aiming to cultivate a generation of environmentally conscious individuals who can effect positive
change in their communities.
One of the project's primary objectives is to enhance environmental awareness among young participants regarding the
pressing challenges our planet faces. We aim to accomplish this through educational workshops that engage participants in
interactive sessions focusing on the science behind climate change and its effects on ecosystems and communities. These
sessions will employ non-formal and informal educational methods to create a more engaging and relatable learning
experience. Furthermore, by encouraging participants to identify and discuss specific environmental issues affecting their
local areas, we make the learning process more relevant, allowing them to connect global challenges to their immediate
surroundings.
The project seeks to facilitate skill acquisition and personal development among participants. We will provide opportunities
for young people to develop a range of skills that not only aid them in understanding and addressing environmental issues
but also enhance their overall growth. This objective aligns with youth work practices that prioritize holistic development.
Each participant will engage in workshops and activities designed to promote critical thinking, teamwork and problem-
solving. Collaborative projects, such as creating awareness campaigns or conducting community clean-up initiatives, will
help participants apply what they learn in practical, meaningful ways. Creative expression will also be encouraged, allowing
participants to communicate their understanding of environmental issues through various media, including videos, photos,
art projects and social media campaigns. This creative outlet supports self-expression while helping them convey their
messages effectively.
Another significant objective of the project is to create collaboration among young people from different cultures and
backgrounds. This aspect is crucial in youth work practice, as it helps break down social and geographical barriers.
Participants will engage in team-based activities designed to promote cooperation and teamwork, building appreciation for
diverse perspectives and the ability to work effectively with individuals from various cultural backgrounds. Cultural exchange
nights will provide opportunities for participants to share their customs, traditions and environmental practices, enriching
their learning experience and cultivating mutual respect.
We are committed to equipping participants with the tools necessary to promote ecological actions within their communities,
aligning with the focus of youth work on empowerment and leadership. To achieve this, we will conduct workshops on
advocacy, providing training on how to advocate for environmental policies and initiatives in their local areas. This training
will encompass skills such as public speaking, event organization and effective social media use. Moreover, we will develop
resources and toolkits that participants can utilize to educate their peers and community members about sustainable
practices. These materials will be designed to be user-friendly and accessible, ensuring they can be widely disseminated.
We aim to inspire participants to adopt a more sustainable lifestyles as a direct outcome of the project. Through hands-on
activities like plogging, recycling and upcycling projects and workshops on sustainable development, participants will gain a
practical understanding of the impact of their choices and be encouraged to embrace environmentally friendly habits.
Following the project's conclusion, participants will be motivated to share their knowledge and experiences within their local
communities through workshops, school presentations or social media campaigns that raise awareness about sustainability.
Finally, one of our key objectives is to leverage social media platforms to amplify our message and reach a wider audience.
This is crucial in youth work practice, as social media serves as a primary communication tool for young people today. We
will create a social media campaign managed by participants, enabling them to share their experiences, learnings and the
importance of environmental action, thereby enhancing their digital literacy while empowering them to engage with a
broader audience. Encouraging participants to maintain social media connections post-project will create a supportive
online community.
What activities do you plan to implement? What is the number and profile of the participants involved?
Earth and You(th) has been carefully designed to engage participants in a variety of non-formal and informal learning
activities, creating a collaborative environment that encourages personal growth and collective action. This project will
involve 30 young people from Hungary, Ukraine, Portugal, Greece and Italy, with teams comprising participants aged 18 to
30 years old and including 1 team leader from each country with the minimum of 18 years of age. Additionally, two
facilitators from Hungary will support the program, ensuring effective learning and engagement throughout the project.

Call 2024 Round 3 YOU
KA152-YOU - Mobility of young people (KA152-YOU)
Form ID KA152-YOU-385B050D Deadline (Brussels Time) 08 Oct 2024 12:00:00
The activities planned for this project are diverse, engaging and focused on key themes related to environmental awareness
and sustainability. One of the primary ice-breaking activities, aptly named “Ice Breaking,” will serve as a warm introduction,
allowing participants to share their backgrounds and aspirations in a relaxed and inclusive atmosphere. This will not only
help establish connections among participants but will also set the tone for an open and supportive learning environment.
Following this initial engagement, participants will partake in discussions designed to facilitate deeper reflections, such as
“Fears, Expectations, Challenges, Self-Reflection.” These discussions will empower participants to voice their concerns and
expectations, creating an inclusive dialogue that values every individual’s perspective. Another reflective session,
“Reflection Time,” will provide a platform for participants to share their experiences and thoughts about the program and the
activities carried out, ensuring that everyone feels heard and appreciated.
Thematic activities will play a pivotal role in achieving the project's objectives. For instance, the “Plogging” activity will
include guided excursions where participants will experience nature first-hand and clean it also. This immersive experience
is designed to enhance their understanding of the local ecosystem and the importance of biodiversity, helping them
recognize the intrinsic value of preserving natural habitats.
Another significant component of the program will be the “Recycling” and "Upcycling" activities. In this hands-on initiative,
participants will recycle and upcycle different materials, thereby actively engaging in efforts to combat environmental
pollution. This activity serves a dual purpose: it provides tangible environmental benefits while creating a sense of social
responsibility and community engagement among participants. This not only highlights the importance of waste
management but also encourages participants to think critically about their consumption habits and the lifecycle of materials.
To assess the effectiveness of these activities and their impact on participants, we will implement several evaluation
methods. Feedback will be collected through surveys and reflection sessions at various stages of the project. Participants
will be encouraged to share their thoughts on the activities, discussing what they learned, how they felt and how these
experiences might influence their future behavior regarding environmental sustainability. We will employ a pre-and post-
project survey to gauge changes in participants' knowledge, attitudes and practices related to environmental issues, which
will provide measurable outcomes that can be analyzed for effectiveness.Qualitative assessments will be conducted through
group discussions and individual reflections. Facilitators will facilitate these sessions to ensure that participants feel
comfortable sharing their experiences. This qualitative feedback will be invaluable in understanding the depth of learning
and the emotional impact of the activities on participants. We will document measurable outcomes such as the volume of
waste collected during the “Polgging” activity, the number of creative items produced from recycled and upcycled materials
and the level of engagement in discussions and workshops. This data will not only serve as an assessment of the project’s
success but also provide insights into areas for improvement in future iterations of the program.
"European Green Deal" workshop will make the participants familiar with the EU created initiative, so following that they can
create their own green policy for the local communities and themselves ("Our Green Policy"). The participants will also be
able to learn new and analyse their habits and they will be equipped with the necessary knowledge to improve their lifestyle
as well as stand up for their beliefs ("Climate Activist Bootcamp").
By combining diverse activities, a supportive group dynamic and comprehensive evaluation methods, the project aims to
create a meaningful and lasting impact on its participants. Through this initiative, we aspire to inspire a generation of
environmentally conscious young people equipped with the skills, knowledge and motivation to champion sustainability
within their communities.
What results and impact do you expect your project to have?
The project aims to create a significant and lasting impact on both the participants and their wider communities, focusing on
environmental awareness and sustainable practices. One of the primary expected outcomes of the project is to enhance the
awareness of young people regarding the environmental challenges our planet faces today. By immersing participants in a
series of educational and practical activities, we anticipate that they will emerge with a deeper understanding of issues such
as pollution, climate change and biodiversity loss. This foundational knowledge will empower them to recognize the
importance of individual and collective actions in addressing these challenges.
The project also aims to equip participants with valuable skills and knowledge through engaging non-formal teaching
methods. By facilitating a learning environment that prioritizes active participation and collaboration, we expect participants
to improve their critical thinking, teamwork and communication skills. These competencies will not only benefit them
personally but also position them as leaders and advocates for environmental sustainability in their communities. Upon
returning home, participants will have the opportunity to apply what they’ve learned, sharing their newfound knowledge and
practices with family, friends and peers. This ripple effect is one of the most significant long-term impacts we anticipate, as
each participant becomes a multiplier of environmental consciousness in their local context.
The project aims to create a sense of community among participants from diverse countries and cultures. Through
collaborative activities, they will learn about each other's backgrounds and perspectives, building intercultural understanding
and solidarity. This experience is expected to lead to lasting friendships and networks that transcend national boundaries,
creating a community of young environmental advocates who support one another in their efforts to promote sustainable
practices. As these participants continue to engage with one another, we foresee them collaborating on future initiatives,
further amplifying the project’s impact.
Community engagement is another critical aspect of the project’s anticipated results. We plan to organize both online and
physical dissemination activities that will extend the project's reach beyond the participants. For instance, workshops,
presentations and community clean-up events will be held to engage local youth and organizations, providing platforms for
participants to share their insights and experiences. These initiatives will not only raise awareness about the environmental

challenges they have studied but also present practical solutions and actions that individuals can incorporate into their daily
lives. By actively involving local community members, we hope to inspire a broader movement toward environmental
stewardship that includes diverse voices and perspectives.
We will collaborate with local organizations, schools and NGOs to maximize the project’s impact. By partnering with
established groups that are already working on environmental issues, we can tap into existing networks and resources to
amplify our message. These collaborations will facilitate the dissemination of our project outcomes, making them accessible
to a wider audience. For example, local NGOs can help organize community events where participants present their
findings, engage in discussions and showcase creative projects produced during the program. These interactions will not
only enrich the local community's understanding of environmental issues but also strengthen ties between youth and
community organizations dedicated to sustainability.
All final outcomes, including videos, photos, articles, our green policy document and other educational materials, will be
shared on social media platforms and the SALTO platform. This strategy ensures that the knowledge and experiences
gained during the project are documented and available for use by other NGOs, youth workers and community members,
supporting ongoing engagement with the topic of environmental sustainability.
The project is designed to achieve both immediate and long-term impacts on participants and their communities. Through
increased environmental awareness, the development of essential skills and the promotion of intercultural collaboration, we
expect participants to become active citizens of change in their localities. By engaging local communities and collaborating
with stakeholders, we aim to create a supportive network that manages sustainable practices and encourages collective
action toward a healthier planet. Ultimately, we envision a future where young people are not only aware of the challenges
facing the environment but are also equipped and motivated to take action, ensuring a brighter, more sustainable future for
all.

have transformed our lives through these projects, developing key competencies, soft skills, and forming connections with
friends across Europe, among many other benefits. We are dedicated to delivering youth work at a quality we would expect
from ourselves, believing that the best way to engage in youth work is through young people working with young people. We
are truly an Erasmus+ generation at Square of Youth Association.
Our mission is to connect Hungarian youth with Europe. We aim to provide opportunities for them to explore Europe,
connect with their European peers, and empower them to become active citizens. We encourage them to take initiatives and
contribute to their communities.
We also aim and working to:
● Help young people learn through non-formal learning activities and enhance their soft skills. Increasing youth participation
through our projects and providing international opportunities for young people.
● Connect youth with stakeholders and decision-makers, facilitating the translation of youth voices to them and increasing
youth involvement.
● Promote dialogue among young people from EU member states and candidate countries, which includes collaborating
with partners from outside the EU as well. Internationality is a key component of our activities because we believe that
international opportunities and mobilities have the potential to improve young people's learning and growth on a high level.
We primarily focus on three key topics, but due to our diversity and ambition, we are open to many other areas as well. The
three key topics are:
● Entrepreneurship and empowering young people to become entrepreneurs. Several of our members became
entrepreneurs at a young age. We believe entrepreneurship not only as a business endeavor but also for its entrepreneurial
skills, which are great soft skills in many aspects of one’s life.
● Active citizenship and youth empowerment. Aiming for our projects and activities can have a multiplier effect and create
impact on a broader scale.
● Topics closely connected to the European Union and being a European citizen.
SOYA is also a member of the Danube Youth Organization Network and is committed to fostering cooperation among
youth-focused organizations in the Danube Region. We find this particularly important in facilitating meetings with decision-
makers and establishing cross-border collaborations.
What are the activities and experience of the organisation in youth work? Please provide information on your organisation’s
/ group’s regular youth work activities.
All the activities we implement are thought, planned and realized by young people.
Regarding the topic of entrepreneurship, we have won the "Entrepreneur in a Week" Erasmus+ youth exchange project,
which will be implemented in spring 2025. Participants will receive all the necessary skills and knowledge to become
entrepreneurs in an intensive week, such as business plan writing, pitching, understanding how to get funding for their
ideas, and more. The project also focuses on developing entrepreneurial skills.
We are currently developing a Visegrad Minigen project: a policy hackathon aimed at young people to explore pathways to
entrepreneurship and identify necessary supports. The project will culminate in a policy document to be presented to
decision-makers and stakeholders.
Integration is also an important topic for us. Currently, we are implementing a Solidarity project in Budapest where we bring
Hungarian and international university students closer together through board games and regular local events.
We organize Erasmus+ information sessions with the goal of democratizing access to Erasmus+. Youth exchanges serve as
a valuable introduction for many young people, yet there are numerous other project types within Erasmus+ – such as
Erasmus for Young Entrepreneurs or European Solidarity Corps (ESC) – that they may not be aware of.
In connection with this initiative, we were exhibiting at the Sziget Festival 2024 to engage naturally with young people,
speaking their language and promoting Erasmus+ and SOYA. Our goal is to attend more festivals to promote Erasmus+,
and non-formal learning, and to inform them about their opportunities.
In addition to our current projects, all of our members have previously participated in numerous Erasmus+ initiatives,
including youth exchanges, training courses, and Erasmus+ semesters at their university. Some of us have also taken part
in European Solidarity Corps volunteer programs, while others have engaged in Erasmus for Young Entrepreneurs. Before
choosing to become actively involved in the program, we were all well-integrated into the Erasmus community and deeply
passionate about its positive impact on our lives.
Please give information on the key staff/persons involved in this application and on the competences and previous
experience that they will bring to the project.
Bence Bíró serves as president and project manager. He holds a Bachelor's degree in Business and Management and a
Master's degree in International Business and Economics. Bence completed an Erasmus+ exchange semester at NOVA
Lisbon Business School. Additionally, he represents Hungary at the Danube Youth Council, advocating for youth in the
Danube macro-region. He was also a representative of Hungary at the COP Youth Section. Bence has completed an
internship at the Global Water Partnership and has participated in numerous Erasmus+ youth exchanges and training
courses.
Gergely Márk Monostori serves as vice president and project manager. He holds Master's degrees in Sports Management
and Finance, along with a Bachelor's degree in International Business. Gergely has represented Hungary at the Global
Youth Forum and has extensive experience with various international student associations. He also has experience in
writing and conducting Erasmus+ projects. His interest areas include non-formal education through sports and outdoor

Please briefly present the organisation / the group
NGO SATELLITE is a non-governmental organization dedicated to empowering and supporting young people aged 13-35
to actively participate in their communities, foster personal development, and contribute to positive social change.
Key Objectives of the organisation:
1.Education and Skill Development: Provide educational programs and workshops to develop essential life skills, including
leadership, communication, and critical thinking.
2.Youth Advocacy: Advocate for the rights and concerns of young people at local, national, and international levels, ensuring
their voices are heard in decision-making processes.
3.Community Engagement: Organize community-based projects and initiatives encouraging youth engagement,
volunteerism, and collaboration with local stakeholders.
4.Health and Well-being: Address issues related to the physical and mental well-being of young individuals through
awareness campaigns, health workshops, and support networks.
5.Cultural Exchange and Diversity: Facilitate cultural exchange programs to promote understanding, tolerance, and
appreciation of diversity among young people.
6.Career Guidance and Entrepreneurship: Offer career guidance and entrepreneurship programs to assist young people in
making informed choices about their future and encourage innovation. 7.Social Innovation: Support and incubate social
innovation projects initiated by young people that address local challenges and contribute to sustainable development.
8.Digital Literacy: Promote digital literacy and technology skills to ensure that young people are equipped to navigate the
digital world and harness its potential for positive impact.
9.Networking and Collaboration: Build a network of young leaders, professionals, and organizations to foster collaboration,
knowledge sharing, and mentorship opportunities.
NGO SATELLITE is the member of international network GYBN and coordinator of the national section of the JEF-Europe in
Ukraine. The organisation is the candidate for joining in National Youth Council of Ukraine.

youth aims to empower the leaders of tomorrow. Our initiative is geared towards the youth, covering the 17 SDGs and
addressing global challenges such as poverty, inequality, and climate change. The program goes beyond theoretical
concepts, encouraging practical application and fostering a sense of responsibility. Participants will develop the skills

required to initiate local projects aligned with the SDGs, promoting a sense of global citizenship and social responsibility.
Through interactive meetings held once a fortnight, participants engage in discussions and collaborate on projects, gaining
a comprehensive understanding of the SDGs and their relevance in shaping a better world.
Speaking Club 'English for All" The Speaking Club is a community-driven initiative that aims to facilitate the development of
confident and practical communication skills among individuals. Through engaging in discussions on a range of topics - from
everyday anecdotes to more complex issues - members of the club can hone their speaking skills while enjoying the
process. One notable aspect of this club is its supportive and collaborative community, which provides an environment
conducive to learning and growth. Additionally, the club offers valuable networking opportunities for members to connect
with like-minded individuals and expand their professional or personal circles. By joining this club, individuals can engage
with youth from diverse regions and collaborate on everyday tasks and exercises while pursuing their academic goals. In
essence, the Speaking Club strikes the perfect balance between learning and fun, enabling members to enhance their
communication skills and enjoy the process of personal development.
YABC trainings (together with URCS volunteers) Youth as Agents of Behavioural Change (YABC) is the IFRC’s long-
standing program that helps people promote a culture of non-violence and peace worldwide. YABC uses non-formal peer
education techniques such as games, role plays, simulation and visualisation exercises, storytelling, and arts. Through
these activities, participants are encouraged to step out of their comfort zone, working together to learn and explore
solutions to problems—all in a safe and supportive learning environment. It starts by fostering self-awareness, self-
reflection, and inner change so that participants can become role models and encourage others to act with respect, equality,
open dialogue, and inclusion. Through the program, participants develop their understanding of different societal
issues—such as discrimination, exclusion, gender, and violence. They also learn valuable social and emotional life
skills—such as empathy, critical thinking, non-violent communication, and negotiation.
Environmental youth trainings “Eco Lifestyle” This project is tailored to the dynamic energy of young minds and combines
the fundamentals of ecology with practical aspects of sustainable living. Participants can delve into interactive sessions that
explore the intricate balance of ecosystems, biodiversity, and the impact of human lifestyles on the environment. The
program is structured to help young individuals understand the importance of environmental conservation and how they can

Our establishment stemmed from a shared vision to empower the youth of Madeira by revitalizing traditions that were at risk

cultural identity.
At the core of our mission lies a commitment to fostering connections and creating opportunities for young people to engage

internationally, we seek to empower the youth of Madeira to take pride in their heritage and become active participants in
shaping the future of our community.

Needs and Objectives
Why do you want to carry out this project? Please describe the issues and needs you want to address and your project’s
objectives.
After some of us being interested since middle school in the problem of climate change and what it involves for all of us, a
project of this kind fell naturally in our mind. Because every one of us is thriving to live in the future, it is important to work for
our environment to be good and comfortable. When talking about climate change, many subjects are involved in the
problem: cause on scale (individual or from big companies), climate justice, activism, wildlife (fauna and flora), pollution,
indigenous’ rights, and more.
The ecological footprint of humanity has been surpassing the carrying capacity of the Earth since the 1980s: its nature’s
supply is not enough to supplement our way of life. It has been warned by scientists since decades, and yet it seems like

Please describe the background of the participants in each participating group and how each group was formed. Please
also provide information on the group leaders, the age of the participants and how country balance is ensured. If
necessary, explain how the gender balance is respected.
The Earth and You(th) project involves participants aged between 18 and 30 from four participating countries: Hungary,
Ukraine, Italy, Portugal and Greece. Our selection process is designed to promote diversity and inclusivity, emphasizing
motivation over specific academic backgrounds or specializations. Each country will contribute to a balanced group, with a
total of 30 participants (including team leaders), ensuring that the project reflects a broad range of experiences and
perspectives.
The formation of groups will be a collaborative effort between our organization and our partner NGOs in each country. Each
partner is tasked with selecting participants based on predefined criteria that prioritize motivation, engagement and
commitment to sustainability and youth work. We recognize the importance of including individuals from various
backgrounds, including those with fewer opportunities. Each participating country will have at least 3 individuals from this
demographic, ensuring their representation and involvement in the project.
The participants will be selected with careful attention to gender balance. We have established guidelines for our partners to
follow, ensuring that both male and female participants are equally represented in the selection process. To achieve this
balance, we will monitor the selection closely and encourage partners to strive for an equal number of male and female
participants.
Participants will be primarily aged between 18 and 30 years, allowing for a vibrant mix of young voices. The inclusion of
team leaders, who must be at least 18 years old with no upper age limit, adds a layer of experience and mentoring to the
group dynamics. Each country will have one designated team leader, who will be responsible for facilitating communication
and supporting participants throughout the project as well as during the preparation by getting familiar with the concept of
an international youth exchange and self-evaluation and goal settings for their personal learning journey.These leaders have
backgrounds in youth work, community development and environmental education, equipping them to create an inclusive
atmosphere. They possess skills in conflict resolution and team building, ensuring participants feel valued. Prior to the
exchange, leaders will attend a preparatory meeting to ensure they are well-prepared to support their teams effectively,
creating a positive and impactful experience for all involved.
To further ensure country balance, each participating country will contribute with equal number of participants and team
leaders. All the countries will be also equally involved in the preparation, implementation and dissemination parts of the
project.
The project will be supported by two facilitators from Hungary with extensive experience in youth work and working with
individuals facing fewer opportunities. These facilitators will play a crucial role in guiding both the participants and the team
leaders, ensuring that everyone is well-prepared for their roles. A preparatory meeting will be held prior to the project to
equip team leaders with the necessary information, clarifying their responsibilities and setting the tone for collaboration and
mutual support. One participant from each partner organization will be also invited to the preparatory meeting, so their voice
can be heard and their needs and fears can be involved in the preparation process.

that everyone is informed and aligned.
Before the Youth Exchange participants will participate in preparatory activities such as international group learning and the
basic idea of the Youth Exchange and the rules and opportunities of it.
Formal communication will also be maintained through emails from both the host and partner organizations. This method will
be utilized for legal matters and important announcements, ensuring that all participants receive consistent and official
information. In addition, the organizations will conduct online meetings both before the Youth Exchange and after it
concludes. These meetings will provide a platform to clarify tasks, outline contributions and address risk prevention
measures, which will be documented in an information pack for participants prior to their departure.
To ensure that participants’ individual needs are met, team leaders will play a crucial role in facilitating communication with
the host organization. They will relay any special dietary requirements or mental well-being considerations to ensure a
supportive environment for all. This process will not only enhance the overall experience for participants but also create a
sense of community and understanding within the group. Team leaders are highly involved from the very beginning of the
project until the end. They will also support their participants through their learning progress by managing and supporting
their dedicated goals for their own development.
As the project progresses into the implementation phase, participants will collaborate in dedicated sessions to plan their
follow-up activities. This will include offline dissemination activities aimed at promoting environmental awareness within their
communities. By allowing participants to define and organize these activities, the project encourages ownership of the
outcomes and reinforces their commitment to sustainability.
Online dissemination will also be a key aspect of the project’s follow-up strategy. Participants will utilize their social media
platforms, including Facebook, Instagram, TikTok to share their experiences and outcomes with a broader audience. They
will be responsible for creating and providing content for these posts, ensuring that their voices are heard and that the
project gains visibility. The host and partner organizations will also disseminate project results, sharing highlights of the
youth exchange as well as the final outcomes, which will be available in multiple languages on platforms like SALTO. This
will not only benefit the participants but also support other NGOs and youth workers seeking educational resources.
The continuous support from team leaders and local organizations will be vital in maintaining momentum throughout the
follow-up phase. These leaders will assist participants in implementing local offline dissemination activities and preparing
final reports that encapsulate their experiences and learning outcomes. After the project concludes, a reflective meeting will
be organized to discuss the overall experience, evaluate the project's impact and explore opportunities for further
collaborative activities.
Ongoing communication between the host organization and partner organizations will ensure a cohesive follow-up process.
Discussions will focus on the dissemination activities conducted online and offline, allowing for feedback and shared insights
that enhance the project's reach and impact. The results of these discussions will be communicated back to the
participants, providing them with a comprehensive overview of how their efforts contributed to a larger narrative of
sustainability and environmental awareness. Through this multifaceted approach to cooperation and communication, the
project will not only foster individual growth but also build a strong network of environmentally conscious youth across
Europe.

Project Design
Preparation, support and follow-up
How will you prepare the participants before the start of the activity (e.g. intercultural, linguistic, risk-prevention etc.) and
how will you support them during and after the activity?
Preparing participants for the project involves a comprehensive approach to ensure they are well-equipped for the activities,
both culturally and linguistically, while also prioritizing their safety and well-being throughout the entire process.
Before the project commences, each partner organization will conduct a series of preparatory meetings with their national
groups. These meetings are critical for organizing logistics, including travel arrangements, insurance, safety measures and
guided cultural visits. During these sessions, participants will receive an extensive project information pack. This pack will
be finalized collaboratively during a preparatory meeting with partners. Team leaders and participants will include vital
details such as local cultural introductions, project objectives, ticket information, insurance coverage, safety awareness and
emergency contact numbers.
Once the selection process is fully completed, chosen participants will fill out a consent form to share their personal data,
including email addresses, adhering to EU-based GDPR regulations to ensure their privacy. Participants will also complete
a dietary requirements form to respect individual dietary habits, including religious dietary restrictions. Following this, they
will be added to WhatsApp and Facebook groups set up by the hosting organization. These groups will facilitate real-time
communication and support, allowing participants to ask questions and seek clarification on any concerns before the project
begins.
Recognizing that the working language of the project is English, a basic proficiency will be required, but advanced speakers
and team leaders will be present to assist those who may need help, creating an inclusive atmosphere. To break the ice and
build rapport among participants, introductory activities will be organized. Ice-breaking sessions will encourage members to
get to know each other in a relaxed setting, promoting camaraderie and easing any anxieties about engaging in an
international environment.
Cultural Nights will serve as an integral part of the preparation, allowing participants to showcase their cultural heritage
through cooking traditional dishes. This initiative promotes intercultural exchange and helps participants appreciate the
diversity of backgrounds within the group. Additionally, "Mix Country Rooms" will be strategically arranged to ensure
participants from different countries share accommodations, further enhancing intercultural experiences and dismantling
geographical barriers. Gender-specific arrangements will also be maintained to ensure comfort.
A crucial aspect of the preparation process involves the selection of experienced team leaders, who will guide and support
their respective groups throughout the program. These leaders will facilitate discussions, provide assistance and serve as
additional support figures, enhancing the overall experience for the participants.
During the Youth Exchange, participants will engage in reflection time, allowing them to evaluate their personal goals and
track their progress. This self-reflection is essential for contributing to self-awareness and mental well-being. Participants
will be encouraged to share their thoughts, feelings and experiences in a safe and supportive environment. Team leaders
will actively manage their teams, ensuring that participants receive continuous support throughout the various activities.
Additionally, risk-prevention measures will be emphasized, with the dissemination of local emergency contacts such as fire,
ambulance and law enforcement agencies. This information will be critical in ensuring the safety and security of all
participants during their stay.
After the project concludes, the support will continue with follow-up meetings, including an online gathering where
participants can discuss how the experience has impacted their personal growth and encouraged them to adopt more
sustainable lifestyles. This reflective session will also serve as an opportunity for participants to disseminate their learning
on green topics within their communities, sharing insights and information about Erasmus+ opportunities with local
audiences.
Local organizations will assist participants in implementing resources and support for local initiatives and dissemination
efforts. Team leaders will play an essential role in maintaining communication, guiding participants as they translate their
experiences into actionable community projects.
Through this comprehensive preparation and ongoing support system, the project will empower participants not only to
engage meaningfully during the exchange but also to continue their commitment to sustainability and intercultural
understanding long after the program has ended. This structured approach ensures that every participant is not only
prepared to learn but is also equipped to share their insights and initiatives within their local contexts, creating a lasting
impact.
What measures will you put in place to ensure the safety and protection of participants?
Ensuring the safety and protection of participants is of paramount importance and a comprehensive set of measures will be
implemented to create a secure environment throughout the entire duration of the exchange. These measures will
encompass various aspects, including accommodation, transportation, emergency contacts, health considerations and
mental well-being.
The selection of the accommodation facility is grounded in stringent safety criteria. The chosen venue is situated in a
serene, natural setting, deliberately located outside the city to facilitate a deeper immersion into the natural environment.
However, it is also strategically close to essential services, such as a hospital and a well-stocked pharmacy, ensuring that
medical assistance is readily available if needed. The facility itself will be inspected to ensure that it meets basic safety
standards, including appropriate fire exits, unobstructed pathways and air circulation in meeting rooms.

Call 2024 Round 3 YOU
KA152-YOU - Mobility of young people (KA152-YOU)
Form ID KA152-YOU-385B050D Deadline (Brussels Time) 08 Oct 2024 12:00:00
To guarantee the safety of participants during their journeys to various activity locations, organizers will carefully plan
transportation arrangements. All participants will travel as a group using public transportation, which not only creates a
sense of community but also enhances security. Each transfer will be accompanied by a local native speaker who is familiar
with the area, providing guidance and support throughout the journey. This measure will help mitigate any potential risks
associated with navigating an unfamiliar environment.
Prior to departure, participants will be required to fill out a health form that includes information about any medications they
are currently taking. This allows organizers to be prepared and ensures that suitable medications can be sourced in the host
country if necessary. Participants will also be informed about the importance of carrying their prescribed medications and
any essential health supplies during their stay as the ones in Hungary might differ from the ones in their home countries.
Each participant will receive a comprehensive list of emergency contacts, including the main organizers' contact information
and local emergency services such as the fire brigade, ambulance and police. This information will be clearly communicated
in the project information pack and displayed in multiple locations within the venue, including the conference room and
common areas. This ensures that all participants are aware of whom to contact in case of any emergencies.
All activities will be conducted with a strong emphasis on safety. For theoretical discussions and workshops, meeting
spaces will be chosen for their size and layout, ensuring unobstructed pathways and proper ventilation. In practical
activities, such as plogging, a guide will be present to oversee the participants. They will provide clear instructions on safety
practices and ensure that no hazardous tools are utilized. Participants will also be equipped with appropriate safety gear,
such as gloves, to minimize risks.
The project recognizes that safety extends beyond physical well-being to encompass mental health. Activities designed to
support relaxation and self-reflection will be integrated into the schedule, including sessions focused on reconnecting with
nature and mindfulness. Participants will have dedicated free time to unwind, complemented by breaks that include
beverages and snacks to nourish both body and mind. The program will also feature reflection sessions, allowing
participants to assess their experiences, track personal growth and engage in discussions about mental health and well-
being.
To further ensure participant safety, comprehensive health and travel insurance will be a requirement for all participants.
Organizers will assist participants in obtaining the best insurance options, including the option of a European Health
Insurance Card (EHIC) for those from applicable countries. This measure provides an added layer of security, ensuring
access to necessary healthcare services during the exchange.
A well-stocked first aid kit will be maintained at the project venue and will accompany the group during outdoor activities.
This preparedness ensures that any minor injuries can be treated promptly. Additionally, a vehicle will be available for
emergencies, facilitating swift transportation to medical facilities if required.
By implementing these extensive safety and protection measures, the project aims to create a secure, supportive
environment where participants can focus on their learning, growth and engagement with nature without unnecessary
concerns about their safety or well-being. The combined efforts of the organizers, team leaders and local support services
will ensure that all participants feel safe, protected and empowered to fully immerse themselves in the transformative
experience of the youth exchange.
What activities are foreseen after the end of the Youth Exchange? How will the participants follow-up on the activity?
After the Youth Exchange, a series of thoughtfully planned follow-up activities will be implemented to ensure that
participants can reflect on their experiences, disseminate knowledge and continue to engage with the themes of
sustainability and Erasmus+ opportunities. These activities aim not only to solidify the learning gained during the exchange
but also to extend its impact within the participants’ local communities.
One of the primary post-exchange activities involves participants actively sharing their newly acquired knowledge regarding
sustainability and Erasmus+ opportunities with their local communities. Each participating organization will facilitate local
dissemination activities, where participants will present the skills and insights they gained during the project. This could
involve workshops, community events or presentations at schools and other local NGOs. Through these discussions,
participants will talk about their intercultural experiences and how the project has encouraged a healthier and more
sustainable lifestyle. By engaging local communities in this way, participants not only solidify their own learning but also
inspire others to consider sustainable practices.
Participants will be given a platform to propose ideas for future projects based on their experiences during the exchange.
Group leaders will welcome and discuss these proposals, creating an environment where participants can brainstorm and
collaborate on potential initiatives. This collaborative approach allows participants to test and apply the skills they acquired
during the exchange while also encouraging them to take ownership of their learning journey and contribute actively to future
endeavors.
To ensure that the outcomes of the project are accessible to a wider audience, the final results—including the main policy
proposal for sustainability—will be translated into all participating languages. This content will be uploaded online for free
access, allowing individuals and organizations beyond the project participants to benefit from the learnings. Additionally,
these outcomes will be shared on SALTO and with our local and international partners.
Throughout the project, participants will capture their experiences through photos and videos, which they can post on
various social media platforms, including Facebook, Instagram, TikTok and YouTube. By sharing their activities and
reflections online, participants can engage their peers and local communities, effectively disseminating the project’s
message and promoting the importance of sustainability and cultural exchange. The use of social media as a tool for
outreach will help create a ripple effect, encouraging more individuals to engage with the themes explored during the youth
exchange.

An essential aspect of the follow-up process will involve an online meeting with partner organizations to evaluate the project
and its outcomes. During this meeting, participants will reflect on their experiences, discuss the effectiveness of the activities
and provide feedback on the overall organization of the project. This collaborative evaluation will help identify strengths and
areas for improvement, ensuring that future youth exchanges can build on the lessons learned from this project.
The support from local NGOs and team leaders will continue after the youth exchange, as they will assist participants in
implementing their local dissemination activities. This support may include providing resources, facilitating connections with
other organizations and guiding participants through reporting duties. By ensuring that participants have access to ongoing
support, organizations can help sustain the momentum generated during the project and encourage participants to remain
active in promoting sustainability.
The exchange will manage the development of a network among participants and partner organizations. This network can
facilitate further collaboration on projects, sharing of ideas and mutual support in sustainability efforts. Participants will be
encouraged to stay connected through the social media groups established before the exchange, allowing them to continue
exchanging ideas, resources and best practices long after the project has concluded.
Through these follow-up activities, participants will not only reinforce their learning from the youth exchange but also
empower themselves and their communities to embrace sustainable practices and explore Erasmus+ opportunities. By
actively engaging in knowledge dissemination, project proposal development and continued collaboration, participants will
extend the reach and impact of the project, ensuring that its lessons resonate far beyond the duration of the exchange.

Recognition of learning outcomes
How will you support participants to be aware of what they have learned and which competences they have developed or
improved? Please remember to include the methods that support reflection and documentation of the learning outcomes in
the daily timetable of each activity.
Supporting participants in recognizing and documenting their learning outcomes is crucial for ensuring they fully appreciate
the competencies they have developed throughout the Youth Exchange. By integrating structured reflection sessions,
documentation processes and the use of the Youthpass certificate, we aim to create an environment that encourages self-
awareness, goal setting and personal growth.
Participants will be introduced to the concept of Youthpass as an essential recognition tool for their non-formal learning
experiences. The Youthpass certificate serves as a formal acknowledgment of their participation and emphasizes the
importance of self-evaluation and personal development. Participants will be guided through the eight key competences
outlined in Youthpass, which include communication in the mother tongue, communication in foreign languages,
mathematical competence and basic competences in science and technology, digital competence, learning to learn, social
and civic competences, sense of initiative and entrepreneurship and cultural awareness and expression. This foundational
understanding will set the stage for their reflective practice throughout the exchange.
On the first day, we will facilitate a group discussion where participants can express their expectations regarding the project
and their personal goals. This will provide an opportunity for individuals to identify specific competencies they hope to
improve. By articulating their interests and objectives, participants will create a personal roadmap for the exchange, making
them more aware of the skills they aim to develop. This initial dialogue will be documented, providing a reference point for
participants to revisit their goals throughout the project.
At the end of each activity day, a dedicated "Reflection Time" will be implemented. This session will involve group
discussions to summarize the day’s activities, both theoretical and practical. Participants will be encouraged to share their
thoughts on what they learned, the relevance of the activities to their personal goals and how these experiences contribute
to their overall development. This reflective practice will promote critical thinking and self-awareness, allowing participants
to evaluate their learning journey in real-time.
During these sessions, participants will break into smaller groups to discuss specific guiding questions related to their
experiences that day. Each day will feature different questions that encourage deeper reflection, such as:
- What new skills did you practice today?
- How did today’s activities relate to your personal goals?
- What challenges did you face, and how did you overcome them?
- How do you plan to apply what you’ve learned in your local context?
Following the small group discussions, the larger group will reconvene to share insights, creating a sense of community and
support. This process not only documents participants' thoughts but also promotes peer feedback, which is essential for a
holistic learning experience.
To further encourage self-reflection and documentation, each participant will be provided with a personal learning journal.
Throughout the project, participants will be encouraged to write about their daily experiences, thoughts and feelings
regarding their learning outcomes. This journaling process serves as a personal archive of their development and can be
revisited to evaluate growth over time. Participants will be prompted to include specific references to the key competences
they are focusing on, reinforcing the connection between their daily activities and their overarching goals.
On the final day of the project, participants will gather for a concluding session where they will reflect on their overall

Environmental friendly practices
Will you include sustainable and environmental-friendly practices in your activities?
Yes
Please describe them and mention how will you raise the awareness of participants on these sustainable practices?
In an era where environmental consciousness is paramount, our project is dedicated to promoting sustainable and
environmentally friendly practices throughout all stages of the program. Recognizing that sustainability is not merely a
concept but a way of life, we aim to instill this philosophy in participants through a combination of theoretical knowledge and
practical activities that emphasize sustainability and environmental stewardship.
Sustainability will be a core theme woven throughout the program, highlighted in both theoretical sessions and practical
activities. Participants will engage in educational workshops that provide insights into sustainability issues, including
biodiversity, climate change and waste management. These sessions will be designed to provoke critical thinking and
inspire participants to consider their roles in managing sustainable practices within their communities.
The most hands-on approaches to learning about sustainability will be through plogging, recycling and upcycling activities
where participants will collect, separate trash and give a new form of using to them in an artistic and self expressing way.
This not only enhances their understanding of nature but also emphasizes the importance of green spaces in urban
environments. By going through the process, they will learn about the different methods of recycling and upcycling which
contribute to a more sustainable ecosystem.
Transportation to various activities will be organized to reduce our carbon footprint. We will encourage participants to travel
in groups, which will significantly decrease the number of vehicles required and consequently lower CO2 emissions. For
shorter distances, we will promote walking as a sustainable travel option. This not only reduces environmental impact but
also allows participants to appreciate their surroundings while promoting physical well-being.
Regarding food waste, we aim to address this by implementing practices that minimize waste during the project. Meal
portions will be carefully planned to prevent excess food while ensuring participants are adequately nourished. We will
support the idea of taking more rounds for more portions then once and leaving it on the plate as food waste.
Raising awareness about waste management will be another aspect of the program. We will implement a system for
separate waste collection, encouraging participants to sort their waste into recyclable and non-recyclable materials. To
further minimize our environmental footprint, single-use plastics will be completely banned from the exchange. Instead,
participants will be encouraged to bring reusable items, such as mugs and cups and we will provide alternatives to plastic
during all meals and activities.
During creative workshops, we will exclusively use recycled materials, thereby creating a culture of reusability and creativity
among participants. By demonstrating how everyday materials can be repurposed into artistic creations, we hope to inspire
participants to think critically about their consumption habits and explore ways to integrate sustainability into their daily lives.
To minimize paper use, we will utilize digital platforms for sharing information and documentation. For instance, tickets for
activities will be uploaded to a shared Google Drive, eliminating the need for printed materials. By encouraging participants
to embrace digital alternatives, we further cultivate a mindset that values sustainability and efficiency.
To minimize also the usage of electricity we will clarify with the participants that if they don't use a device or they are not in
the room plug off the device and switch off the lights.
Throughout the program, we will incorporate interactive discussions and activities focused on sustainability. Participants will
have opportunities to share their thoughts and experiences regarding environmental issues, which will not only facilitate
learning but also empower them to take ownership of their impact on the environment.
We will include dedicated workshops that emphasize the importance of sustainability in daily life. These workshops will
provide participants with practical tips and strategies for integrating sustainable practices into their routines. Additionally,
reflection sessions will be held to discuss the significance of these practices, encouraging participants to consider how they
can apply what they've learned in their communities.
We will support green travel options, although the most realistic ways are available just for the Hungarian and Ukrainian
teams because of the distances. All the teams can contribute to the protection of the nature by travelling together.
Participants will be encouraged to collaborate and share their insights on sustainability with one another. This peer-learning
approach not only enhances individual understanding but also creates a supportive environment where participants can
inspire and motivate each other to adopt more sustainable habits.

Erasmus+ Youth Quality Standards
Organisations implementing Erasmus learning mobility activities must adhere to a set of quality standards. These standards
exist to ensure quality experience and learning outcomes for Erasmus participants, and to make sure that all organisations
receiving the Programme’s funding are contributing to its objectives.
I. POLICY PRIORITIES IN THE FIELD OF YOUTH :
Beneficiary organisations should contribute to the overall objectives of the EU Youth Strategy [1] and to the European Youth
Goals developed within this frame. They should apply the guiding principles laid down in the EU Youth Strategy.
II. BASIC PRINCIPLES OF THE ERASMUS PROGRAMME :
• Inclusion and diversity: beneficiary organisations must respect the principle of inclusion and diversity in all aspects
of their activities. Whenever possible, beneficiary organisations should open their activities to participants from
different backgrounds and with diverse abilities, actively engage and involve participants with fewer opportunities.
• Environmental sustainability and responsibility: beneficiary organisations must make efforts to design activities
in an environmentally friendly way and promote environmentally sustainable and responsible behaviour among its
participants.
• Virtual cooperation, virtual mobility and blended mobility: beneficiary organisations should integrate the use of
digital tools and learning methods to complement the physical mobility activities or to improve the cooperation with
partner organisations.
• Active participation in the network of Erasmus organisations: beneficiary organisations should seek to
become active members of the Erasmus network by taking part in activities organised by the National Agency or
other organisations. Experienced Erasmus beneficiaries should share their knowledge with organisations who have
no or little experience in the programme, providing them with advice, mentorship or other support. Beneficiary
organisations should also encourage their participants to take part in Erasmus community building activities.
III. QUALITY MANAGEMENT
• Responsibility: beneficiary organisations will be responsible for results and quality of the implemented activities,
regardless of the involvement of any other organisations or individuals. During the implementation of Erasmus
activities, beneficiary organisations must take responsibility for key decisions on all tasks that directly affect the
outcomes of the implemented activities, especially in relation to these quality standards. The core tasks include
financial management of the programme funds, contact with the National Agency, reporting on implemented
activities, as well as all decisions that directly affect the content, quality and results of the implemented activities.
• Transparency: beneficiary organisations may receive advice, assistance or services from other organisations or
individuals, as long as it keeps control of the content, quality and results of the implemented activities. If the
beneficiary organisations are using the programme funds to pay for such services, rights and obligations of both
parties (in particular tasks to be carried out, quality control mechanisms and consequences in case of poor or failed
delivery on part of the service provider) must be formally defined and available for review by the National Agency.
• Partnerships: While overall responsibility of the implementation lies within the beneficiary organisations, activities
should be planned, implemented and evaluated together with all partner organisations involved. Clear definition of
roles, and distribution of tasks should be set-up and agreed.
• Contributions paid by participants: as a form of co-funding, the beneficiary organisation may ask participants in
activities for contributions to pay for goods and services necessary for the implementation of those activities. The size
of the participants’ contributions must remain proportional to the grant awarded for the implementation of the activity,
must be clearly justified, collected on a non-profit basis, and may not create unfair barriers to participation (especially
concerning participants with fewer opportunities). Additional fees or other participant contributions cannot be
collected by other service providers chosen by the beneficiary organisation.
• Monitoring and evaluation: beneficiary organisations should put in place adequate plans and procedures to
monitor and evaluate the activities, to enable them to assess how they are progressing towards their objectives and
whether they have reached them. The monitoring and evaluation outcomes should lead to improvements in the
implementation of ongoing or future activities.
• Building capacity and integrating results of the activities in the organisation: beneficiary organisations
should take steps to use the results of the implemented activities in their regular activities, to build their capacity and
the capacity of their staff.

IV. QUALITY LEARNING ACTIVITIES
This section sets the minimum standards to follow. Further guidance and tools are available to support the delivery of high
quality learning mobility activities, such as the Quality Framework for Learning Mobility.
• Practical arrangements: beneficiary organisations must ensure quality of practical and logistic arrangements
(travel, accommodation, insurance, safety, visa applications, social security, etc.). If these tasks are delegated to the
participants or another participating organisation, beneficiary organisations will remain ultimately responsible for
verifying their provision and quality.
• Health, safety and respect of applicable regulation: all activities must be organised with a high standard of
safety and protection for involved participants and must respect all applicable regulation (for example regarding
parental consent etc.). The beneficiary organisations must ensure that their participants have appropriate insurance
coverage, as defined by the general rules of the Programme and the applicable regulation.
• Selection of participants: participants must be selected through a transparent, fair and inclusive selection
procedure.
• Preparation and support of participants: participants must receive appropriate preparation in terms of practical,
cultural and safety aspects. They should be fully aware of what is expected from them before, during and after the
activity. Adequate guidance and support must be available during all phases of the activities.
• Support to participants with fewer opportunities: beneficiary organisations must ensure fair and equal
conditions for all participants, including those facing barriers (such as special learning needs or physical disability).
• Participatory approach: participants should - as far as possible - be actively involved in all phases of the activities
and receive adequate support to do so.
• Participatory methods: activities should be based on participatory methods and offer space for interaction of
participants, sharing of ideas, avoiding passive listening and allow them to contribute to the activities with their own
knowledge and skills, reversing the traditional roles of outside "experts".
• Linguistic support: beneficiary organisations must ensure appropriate language training, adapted to the needs of
the participants. Where appropriate, the beneficiary organisation should make maximum use of the specific tools and
funding provided by the Programme for this purpose.
• Definition and monitoring of learning outcomes: the expected learning outcomes must be clearly defined for
each participant or group of participants prior to the activity. Where relevant, participants should be involved in the
identification of the expected learning outcomes. Progress in the learning process should be monitored and adequate
support should be provided to participants in this regard.
• Identification and recognition of learning outcomes: non-formal and informal learning outcomes gained by
participants in the activities should be identified and documented, in particular through EU level recognition tools
such as Youthpass. Youthpass is a tool to support validation of non-formal and informal learning outcomes. It is a
part of the European Commission's strategy to foster the recognition of non-formal learning. Youthpass
V. Sharing results and knowledge about the programme
• Sharing results within the organisation: beneficiary organisations should make their participation in the Erasmus
programme widely known within the organisation and create opportunities for participants to share their mobility
experience with their peers.
• Sharing results with other organisations and the public: beneficiary organisations should share the results of
their activities with other organisations and the public.
• Publicly acknowledging European Union funding: beneficiary organisations should make their participation in
the Erasmus programme known in their community and in the wider public. Beneficiary organisations must also
inform all participants about the source of the fund.
